storytellers.bio: Keiron Lander is a dynamic cultural advocate deeply rooted in the heritage of the JI people, fostering resilience through his life's journey. Growing up in Western Queensland's tight-knit town of Quipe, Keiron was profoundly shaped by his family's legacy and the nurturing care of his grandmother, who fought to keep him and his twin sister united. His upbringing amidst the vibrant landscapes of Windorah and the values instilled by his uncles set the foundation for his aspirations in sports, ultimately leading him to thrive at Downlands College in Toowoomba. Keiron's life is a testament to the enduring power of determination and community, as he channels his experiences into uplifting and empowering young people.

Aunty Evie is a cherished custodian of family and cultural heritage, known for her vivid storytelling that bridges generations. Raised in a land brimming with familial history, she fondly recounts her childhood treks from One Mile through the bush, echoing the footsteps of her ancestors at Moongalba. Her tales are woven with memories of lush mango and cherry trees, and the heartfelt dedication of her mother riding from Dunwich to care for elders. Aunty Evie’s stories not only preserve the legacy of her grannies and grandfathers but also celebrate the spirit of resilience and connection to the land. Through her narratives, she imparts wisdom and ensures that the rich tapestry of her community's past remains vibrant and alive.

Amelia "Millie" Tynan is a vibrant connector and advocate, known for her unyielding dedication to family and community impact. Born into a sisterhood of five, Millie's formative years were shaped by the dynamic of a bustling household, teaching her resilience and adaptability. After making her mark as an executive assistant in Sydney, Millie returned to Brisbane, drawn by the pull of family and the thriving atmosphere at Younguns. Collaborating with influential figures like Scott and Kieran, she seamlessly transitioned into crucial roles, contributing to initiatives that empower youth. Millie's journey reflects her passion for creating meaningful connections and fostering opportunities in traditionally male-dominated spaces.

Troy John McConnell is a dedicated and inspirational mentor at Young Guns, where he transforms lives by drawing on his personal journey of overcoming challenges to find stability and joy. At 40, Troy’s path has been one of resilience, marked by ups and downs, leading him to embrace a fulfilling role that not only benefits him but also positively impacts his family. Since joining Young Guns a year and a half ago, Troy has become more reliable, motivated, and positive, embodying a newfound happiness that he eagerly shares with others. Through his physically demanding work, Troy not only stays fit but also finds meaning in helping others find their own paths to growth and fulfillment. His future aspirations include continuing this rewarding journey, fostering a supportive community, and making life happier for all involved.

George Nanganjuan is an authentic community leader dedicated to bridging cultural gaps and fostering growth, drawing from his rich Papua New Guinean heritage and upbringing in Rockhampton. Deeply rooted in his love for footy, family, and the value of hard work, George is a straightforward mentor who connects genuinely with young Indigenous men. His journey through Young Guns symbolizes his commitment to creating a supportive environment where cultural understanding meets mentorship. Inspired by his own experiences growing up with diverse friends, George focuses on nurturing good men over mere workers, celebrating personal triumphs like that of Tyson, who transformed from a wandering youth to a crew leader under George’s guidance.

Callum William Mason, a resilient voice from Brisbane and the Atherton Tablelands, has transformed adversity into strength through tenacity and self-reflection. Growing up amidst challenging conditions, Callum navigated a tumultuous childhood marked by poverty and the absence of a father figure. By the age of 12, he witnessed a pivotal change as his family overcame addiction, which inspired him to alter his path and embrace positive influences. Despite early interactions with the justice system and educational setbacks, Callum's unique perspective on youth culture and environment informs his commitment to change. Now involved with West Jobs through Centrelink, he is dedicated to fostering personal growth and supporting others in their journey toward fulfilling careers.

keyMoments.text: I've been around boys like this my whole life, so that's why get the lingo and stuff like that.
keyMoments.significance: George's cultural connection to Aboriginal youth

keyMoments.text: We want to create good men, not really good workers. So that's the first thing for us.
keyMoments.significance: George's focus on personal growth over work skills

keyMoments.text: I think if corporations are, like, if big companies, they wanna invest in these young fellas, if they can understand why it's hard for them to find jobs and why it's hard for them to create something for themselves it could go a long way.
keyMoments.significance: George's message to businesses about understanding the struggles of Aboriginal youth

keyMoments.text: I've always said if you can make it in container, you can make it anyway.
keyMoments.significance: George's metaphor for perseverance and resilience

keyMoments.text: I think about, it's about all about understanding what they go through. I like the idea what you said about the photos. With a lot of the huge crime and stuff like that.
keyMoments.significance: George's reflection on systemic issues facing Aboriginal youth

keyMoments.text: I've had a very good life. And being around these kids is very eye-opening and I feel super privileged to be in the room with them and learn all about those stories.
keyMoments.significance: Amelia's reflection on her own privilege and gratitude for being part of the program

keyMoments.text: It's everyone's problem. You all sit there and say how, prime's up and all of these things. It's will help us do something about it.
keyMoments.significance: Amelia's call to action for non-Indigenous businesses and individuals to support the program

keyMoments.text: It's very much like we want you to succeed. That's the only reason that we're here. That's the reason why job exists. So that's what we want for you.
keyMoments.significance: Amelia's emphasis on the program's commitment to supporting participants' success

keyMoments.text: I think it is about a bunch of guys that gave it a go that are willing to give another bunch of guys a go.
keyMoments.significance: Amelia's explanation of the program's purpose and values

keyMoments.text: We provide, and definitely once they're in work, this cohort that's just gone through their. Five weeks in, and we're still calling them almost every time they have a shift to make sure that they're showing up.
keyMoments.significance: Amelia's description of the program's wraparound support

analysis.emotionalTone: Reflective, humble, and enthusiastic with a sense of privilege and gratitude

capability.capacity_built: Contributing to employment outcomes for disadvantaged youth

option_value.commons_contribution: Demonstrates how non-indigenous staff can meaningfully contribute to Indigenous employment outcomes

community_value_return.direct_benefits: Employment pathways and ongoing support for participants and their families

warnings: High turnover requires constant reeducation on cultural awareness
It's easy to get people through programs but hard to keep them engaged
innovations: Six-week model with four weeks on-site experience
Calling participants before each shift to ensure attendance
Custodian economy concept connecting like-minded businesses
lived_experience: Mentorship through ongoing check-in calls maintains engagement post-placement
Understanding environments prevent punitive responses to mistakes
Family impact is the true measure of long-term success

themes: Resilience in the face of adversity
Breaking cycles of poverty and addiction

keyMoments.text: I was getting worried about that. I just really tried to focus on run my role models, my good role models, and during that time where you were struggling, do you have any understanding of like. Why, do you think, was it like a mix of poverty, family, like what you Yeah, just bad influences, friends, poverty stuff of that nature really.
keyMoments.significance: Highlights the struggle with identity and belonging

keyMoments.text: I think it's just the environment you're brought up in, to be honest. And yeah, if you get brought up in a bad environment and you have all these other influences of doing the same thing around you, you tend to lean towards that.
keyMoments.significance: Exposes the impact of environmental factors on behavior

keyMoments.text: I hope the program keeps going. I think it helps out a lot of young indigenous people and I have family members that have, come outta prison and it's hard for them to find work, because, they do police checks at a lot of places and they'll judge you from that.
keyMoments.significance: Highlights the importance of supportive programs

authority.cultural_positioning: Indigenous youth with direct experience of the challenges and support systems described

capability.capacity_built: Demonstrated self-teaching capability in programming; work readiness; aspiration to help others in similar situations

future_applications: peer mentoring for other Indigenous youth
option_value.commons_contribution: Shared experience of navigating non-judgmental support; model for other Indigenous youth considering similar pathways

community_value_return.capacity_building: Workplace skills, self-confidence, technology skills, resume building

warnings: Police checks and background screening create barriers for ex-prisoners seeking employment
Environmental factors and lack of positive influences contribute to youth offending
lived_experience: Navigating poverty and family addiction as a young Indigenous person
Experience of school expulsion and the school-to-prison pipeline risk
Breaking intergenerational cycles through personal agency
Benefits of non-judgmental employment support for Indigenous youth
Self-directed technology learning without formal training

stories.title: Amelia Tynan
stories.summary: Amelia Tynan, known as Millie, works as part of Kieran's team at Young Guns after three and a half years as executive assistant. She describes Young Guns as a potential-first program where leaders who took risks themselves now create safe spaces for young Indigenous people to find their own way. The program provides intensive wraparound support including ongoing mentorship after employment placement, with the goal of sustained engagement that creates intergenerational impact on families. Amelia emphasizes that true scaling requires buy-in from non-Indigenous businesses and individuals, not just Indigenous organizations and government - calling out the gap between having RAP plans and taking real action. She sees the six-week pathway structure and understanding approach to setbacks as key to building trust and connection.
stories.content: Amelia Tynan, known as Millie, works as part of Kieran's team at Young Guns after three and a half years as executive assistant. She describes Young Guns as a potential-first program where leaders who took risks themselves now create safe spaces for young Indigenous people to find their own way. The program provides intensive wraparound support including ongoing mentorship after employment placement, with the goal of sustained engagement that creates intergenerational impact on families. Amelia emphasizes that true scaling requires buy-in from non-Indigenous businesses and individuals, not just Indigenous organizations and government - calling out the gap between having RAP plans and taking real action. She sees the six-week pathway structure and understanding approach to setbacks as key to building trust and connection.

stories.title: George Nanganjuan
stories.summary: George Nanganjuan, a Papua New Guinean man who grew up in Rockhampton among Aboriginal communities, shares his experience as a mentor with Young Guns First. He supervises young Aboriginal men doing physically demanding container unloading work for Kmart, but emphasizes that the program's true goal is 'creating good men, not really good workers.' George's deep understanding of the struggles facing Aboriginal youth - gained through lifelong friendships and shared community experience - informs his compassionate, relationship-based mentorship approach. He shares the transformation of Tyson, who went from housing instability to becoming a crew leader. George advocates for corporate understanding of the systemic barriers these young men face, arguing that empathy and opportunity - not punishment - are what's needed. His approach prioritizes attitude and character over skills, believing that 'if you can make it in container, you can make it anyway.'

George's deep understanding of the struggles facing Aboriginal youth - gained through lifelong friendships and shared community experience - informs his compassionate, relationship-based mentorship approach. He shares the transformation of Tyson, who went from housing instability to becoming a crew leader. George advocates for corporate understanding of the systemic barriers these young men face, arguing that empathy and opportunity - not punishment - are what's needed. His approach prioritizes attitude and character over skills, believing that 'if you can make it in container, you can make it anyway.'

stories.title: Callum William Mason
stories.summary: Callum William Mason shares his journey from childhood poverty and behavioral challenges in Brisbane and North Queensland (Atherton Tablelands) to stable employment through the Young Guns program. After recognizing the intergenerational pattern of incarceration in his family, he consciously chose a different path. Through West Jobs/Centrelink, he connected with Young Guns, completed a six-week course including forklift certification, and found meaningful employment. He values the non-judgmental hiring approach that doesn't focus on background checks, recognizing this as crucial for Indigenous people with justice system involvement. Callum is self-taught in multiple programming languages since age 13 and plans to pursue technology studies while building his work resume. He works for his family and advocates for the program's continuation to help other young Indigenous people facing similar barriers.
